text: Does the aero automatically switch modes in these zones or is it enabled and drivers may/must toggle the modes? From sporting regulations B7.1.2 a) The driver may only activate, fully or partially, the Driver Adjustable Bodywork when they have been notified via the Control Electronics (Article C8.2) that it is enabled. So it's fully under driver control. Are there sensors that set which mode is allowed or is it based on regulation and a driver could theoretically switch modes where not allowed and incur a penalty? There are only 2 modea defined, Straight line mode and cornering mode in the standard ECU and software that provides the signal. Why not allow it everywhere, is it a safety issue or something else? Hust like with DRS in the past, where it was allowed everywhere around the circuit during FP sessions, this led to drivers trying to maximize the DRS effect, regularly losing control and spinning out or at worst flipping the car. Imagine taking a high angle corner with no downforce - your car will either spin out based on minimum input or just not turn in. That's why one mode is called straight line mode (allowing maximum top speed with reduced drag) and cornering mode (to be able to carry most speed through a corner).
label: analysis

text: The layout of the buttons and paddles is usually standardised, although Williams made a totally custom wheel for Kubica, and Hamilton always used a different clutch padel setup at Mercedes compared to Bottas, so customisation is possible. text: Mercedes had a lot of sunk cost fallacy going on with the zeropods. They weren't necessarily conservative with it, just weren't willing to throw away all that development time on a concept they were convinced was always one upgrade away from working.
label: analysis

text: I believe the philosophy behind it was sound, making it work in practice though was much more complicated, with the zeropod you had more of the surface area of the floor exposed to the airstream, meaning you can potentially extract more downforce, the main problem was the drag (because of the more exposed rear tyres and suspension components) and sealing the floor (to prevent porpoising).
label: analysis

text: The issue wasn't necessarily sealing the floor itself (it was actually quite good at that, even too good) but more that Mercedes assumed they could control their rear ride heights a lot better than they actually could.
label: analysis

text: Yeah, TLDR was that the zeropod concept was the fastest CONCEPT but it had the smallest window. If it worked, it’d be the fastest by far. BUT, and the reason why no one else went with it, was because the operating window was so tiny. It only worked in a very very small window and when not in that window, it was, well, the Mercedes. The other top teams went for a lower ceiling but wider window so they could reliably and consistently get everything out of the car
label: analysis

text: Considered but crucially, ultimately dumped. That whole idea fell apart pretty brutally hard (not just the zeropods, admittedly) once the rubber met the road. Of course a Venturi car is going to be REALLY fast if you've got an absolute fuckload of floor surface and you run it extremely close to the track. The problem is rear ride height control and Mercedes could never figure that out. I don't think there was any figuring that out without straight up active suspension, though.
label: analysis

text: People seem to forget mercs biggest problem wasn’t the zero pod or aero but the chassis and suspension causing issues that they couldn’t figure out because the simulations weren’t accurate.
label: analysis

text: They actually talked about this during that season. They didn’t know their simulations weren’t working at the time so took everything back to basically zero to start fresh and eliminate all variables. After that they realized what their problem was.
label: analysis

text: It also took them till 2020 to move the side crash structure forward of the sidepod inlets and to place the sidepod inlets as high as possible, which Ferrari did in 2017 and most other teams copied in 2018.
label: analysis

text: Not sure that one thing is proof of them being conservative, especially when they've been known for taking plenty of risks that paid off elsewhere by going down a noticeably different path than their competition. Just a few examples: the W-duct in 2011 (not a massive success for them but it did have enough benefits that RB copied it at the end of that year before it got banned); their nose and front suspension in 2014; their version of FRIC in 2014 that was so advanced that their competition successfully lobbied for it to be banned just 9 races into the season; being the only team to make the low rake and long wheelbase concepts work from 2017 onwards (with Ferrari themselves moving towards the long wheelbase in 2018); the swept-back rear suspension in 2020 (which RB spent a development token on to copy in 2021); the "canon shoulders" on the B-spec W14 (which Red Bull adopted on their RB20 the following year). Not to mention that from 2019 to 2021, teams started copying some of their aerodynamic solutions regarding the nose, front wing and bargeboards. If there's one area where Merc has never really been conservative, it's car development, quite the opposite in fact
label: analysis

text: I think that was also partially a hang over from their dominant phase. There was a series of articles on the BBC by someone called secret F1 aerodynamicst, widely believed to be James Allison, who talked about how Mercedes always went against the grain in the old regulation set pre ground effect which obviously worked very well for them then. The primary example was Mercedes’ low rake philosophy and how the entire grid pursued the Newey lead high rake philosophy. I think Mercedes was just hellbent on sticking to that kind of philosophy in the ground effect era too hoping it would magically click

text: I remember an interview with Patrick Head where he talked about getting their first engine from Honda This would have been in '83, as they were building their first Honda-powered car for '84 (which they actually ran in the final race of '83) He said that an engine just arrived in a box from Japan No data sheets, nothing, just an engine He needed heat rejection data so they could design and specify the cooling system for the hot turbo engine (up to this point the team had been running Cosworth DFV). Obviously there needed to be adequate cooling to keep the engine reliable, but not too much as that added weight (radiators too big etc) So he asked Honda and they just said "work it out for yourself" It was acceptable in the 80s, but not 40+ years later
label: analysis

text: Did you account for repeated measures in your MANOVA? If you have qualifying position and race result both as dependent variables in the same model (which you don't specify, but you only list one MANOVA p-value) , you have to account for the fact that one may have an effect on the other, so you have to have a repeated measures correction using something like mixed effects models in lme4. That could affect significance.
label: analysis

text: I highly doubt he's doing a multivariate linear mixed effects model for this... Even if he is, the LMM would have to rely quite a bit on partial pooling which would shrink the estimates quite severely since the data is pretty limited. I'm not even going to get into the assumptions of this model including normality, constant variance (with a Frequentist framework even modelling sigma is hard and pretty much impossible with this amount of data, etc. It's a meme analysis but I always get concerned since a ton of poeple may not understand that.
label: analysis

text: I don't want to completely write off the level of success that Red Bull enjoyed during that period (particularly 2023), but let's also acknowledge that this isn't the most dominant track record in recent F1 history. Mercedes' dominance between 2014-2020 was greater, and over a longer period of time, across driver lineups, than Red Bull's - in no small part because they got more out of both drivers. Over those seven seasons their drivers finished 1-2 in the driver's championship four times. Their average gap to the second placed constructors team was 52%. In only one year (2018) did their second driver finish outside the top three. Similarly, McLaren this year finished 78% over the second placed team in the constructors (833 / 469). Their two drivers finished 1-3. Red Bull, for all their success riding Verstappen's skill, only won two constructors titles between 2021-2024. The two years they failed, their second driver finished P4 (2021) and P8 (2024). Their gap to the second placed team in 2022 was only 37% (759 / 554). So, to answer your question directly: Why are we out here questioning a strategy that netted 4 WDC and 2 WCC? Because another strategy has yielded greater success, over a longer time frame. Teams like Alpine would love to be Red Bull, but I'm not sure teams like Mercedes or McLaren would.
label: analysis

text: It’s a thermodynamics problem primarily. Physical engine displacement, volumetric efficiency, allowable cylinder pressure, allowable fuel flow, number of turbochargers, whether or not they are “e-turbos”, whether or not there is charge air cooling (intercooler/aftercooler) and (if so) the efficiency thereof, etc, are all going to be important variables. Those will bound the combinations that can work and then, they’ll consider the performance/driving characteristics that will drive the turbine and compressor sizes towards the final solution.
label: analysis

text: Engine design and performance. To explain it in top level, Initially, the engine max torque and power limits would be set. For those, inlet air pressure (boost) would be decided. Now turbocharger is designed to meet the boost pressure. Also design may vary due to packaging. Car weight falls within the scope of engine, not turbocharger. since ambient air pressure changes at different altitudes, yes that is an important factor.
label: analysis

text: Honestly I am uncertain if this year turbocharger is that special. They taken out the MGU-H and the MGU-K is there to compensate turbolag. The engine is very lean burning so exhaust gasses aren’t that extremely hot. Wouldn’t be surprised if they bought it or have it custom made by Garett, Ihi, or one of the other big turbo manufacturers.
label: analysis

text: One of the more basic tunes is to run a lower drag & downforce setup with an adjusted rear wing. Faster in the straights but less rear grip through the corners, see if your driver can make it work. It was one of the main things RB was trying through the 2021 season, but much more dramatically illustrated by Lewis at Turkey in the 2006 GP2 race.
label: analysis

text: They almost certainly don’t tune a car to be difficult to drive. They tune it for minimum lap time using their driving skills, which will be a narrow operating envelope that others struggle to consistently stay inside. Similar to modern fighter jets that are inherently unstable in flight and need computers to prevent immediately crashing. But this instability makes them more responsive and able to turn faster.
label: analysis

text: I dont think they intent to make the car "twitchy". The computer / simulator just shows that this combination of setup-changes would in theory create the best laptime, however the car may now be harder to control. This is like the typical 9/10 times you can just go flat above the kerbs but on the 10th time you just have a minimal different line or speed and suddenly you just spin out of control...
label: analysis

text: Not 100% sure where these operating temperature regions came from - back when I was in F1 (3 years ago, used to be resposible for brake material choice and cooling options in weekend plus dyno running) we used CI discs and there different 4 material specs avaialable to us at the time, one that matches the above plot pretty well, but another that worked well as low as 180-200degC and I know this is what my team ran primarily 2023-2024. Beyond that I can't say but I'd be surprised if this wasn't still available as it had a very wide temperature range and good any glazing charicataristics
label: analysis

text: Not a brakes engineer, so I'll stay out of the CI-vs-Brembo temperature debate — but on the lock-up point, that's something you can actually check in the data rather than argue about. Lock-ups show up clearly in telemetry: sharp brake-pressure spike with a sudden drop in wheel speed against car speed. If someone pulled Leclerc's traces for that lap you could see exactly how many there were and how severe, which would help separate "brake disc out of window" from "marbles/asphalt causing lock-ups." The cause might be hard to prove, but the lock-ups themselves aren't a matter of opinion — they're in the data.
label: analysis

text: The graph is almost completely irrelevant, we have no idea how the different discs compare in terms of heat dissipation and thermal mass which is what will dictate (along with the cooling system) how quickly the brakes reach or fall out of operating temperature. Pads used are also going to change the equation. We don't have nearly enough data to make any meaningful analysis.
label: analysis

text: I think it's more likely that their Miami upgrade, which included ERS modifications, affected brake cooling in a more complex way. For a hypothetical example, it could be that the upgrades were designed to brake harder and later to take advantage of the higher temp threshold of CI. Leclerc on his brembos might've been braking too early to such a degree that it fell out of the window entirely. Not to mention the algorithmic ERS that reacts in real time to prioritize energy, that might've not been calibrated separately for 2 district setups.
label: analysis

text: The difference in Hamilton and Leclerc’s paces underscore the value of not being caught in the midfield traffic. Part of the break that Hamilton got was that Russell was concentrated on defensively racing antonelli rather than hunting down Hamilton after Ham’s second tire change. So it allowed Ham to straight drive rather than battle . Based on qualifying and prior experience, I’m sure Merc was under the impressions that Ferrari couldn’t keep up the pace and would eventually fall behind like they usually do. But the Ferrari upgrades worked this race. I can almost guarantee Merc won’t make that mistake next race. Hamilton ended up keeping the same pace as Russell in the beginning of race with Hamilton on used softs and Russell on mediums. In Barcelona! And i wondered if it meant they would eventually battle.
label: analysis

text: Haas is an outlier in the sheer volume of parts that they outsource to dallara. They have the smallest in-house factory setup by far. The rest of the teams are producing most of the cars themselves. Obviously you have the customer teams purchasing their engines, and sometimes their gearboxes and rear suspensions from a supplier team. But the outsourced parts from third parties are fairly small. The brakes are from one of a few brakes companies. A lot of the electronic components are made with technical partners. But the physical chasis of the car is mostly in-house.
label: analysis

text: The rule is works teams do the software, chassis, aerodynamics and engine in house. Customer teams don’t do the software or engine, but they do the chassis and aerodynamics. Haas is an exception as they prefer to buy non-custom parts to design their cars. Things like brakes, suspension and fluids are outsourced but usually custom. If a company has a parts sponsor, it’s outsourced and custom.
label: analysis

text: The ECU (the brain - Electronic Control Unit) is standardized, used by all teams, and is made by McLaren Applied (I think it's now an independent company from McLaren Group), which is again separate from the McLaren F1 Team. "The ECU governs the entire electronic ecosystem of the car, including fuel injection, the gearbox, the clutch, the differential, energy recovery systems, and the Active Aero." "The connection to McLaren extends directly to the steering wheels as well. While every F1 team designs and builds the physical carbon-fiber shell and layout of their own steering wheels, they are required by regulations to use internal electronic components from McLaren Applied."
label: analysis

text: Most of the drivers in 2025 did a two stop strategy with the c2-c3 compounds. it probably won’t be any different this year. This isn't necessarily true. I had the opportunity to ask Mario Isola some questions around the time of the Japanese GP, and he confirmed to me that the new generation of cars put a significant amount less wear into the tyres than the previous generation. That also makes logical sense too of course. The '26 cars are significantly slower in the corners than the '25 cars, but then make up for it down the straights with the active aero. That is always going to put less energy into the tyres. Obviously the compounds have been altered somewhat to account for that, but you can't simply go "well it was a 2-stop with these compounds last year so it should be this year too" following a major rule change like we've just had.
label: analysis

text: the softer compounds force higher degradation, which means nobody's gonna cruise through on a single stop even at a track like barcelona where you'd normally get away with it. pirelli's essentially saying "you have to make a choice here" rather than letting the fastest car just gap everyone and manage tyres for 40 laps. it's a bit of a sledgehammer approach but it works if you want unpredictable strategies. the tradeoff is that drivers spend more time managing rubber that's falling off a cliff instead of actually racing. you get more pit stops, which can shuffle the grid, but you also get more of that careful tyre nursing where the guy in front just inches it round and nobody can really attack. barcelona's already a hard place to overtake, so banking on strategy chaos to save the race is... something.
label: analysis
